items.foco: Classic triad: pain, visual loss, dyschromatopsia
items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad

  • Ocular or periocular pain, exacerbated by eye movements, due to inflammation of the optic nerve.
  • Subacute visual loss (hours to days), typically unilateral, with variable acuity from mild to no light perception.
  • Dyschromatopsia (especially red-green) is an early and sensitive finding; evaluate with the Ishihara test or direct ophthalmoscope red.
  • Trap: the absence of pain does not rule out optic neuritis, but should alert us to anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION).

items.title: Association with MS
items.contentHtml:
Most common association: multiple sclerosis

  • Multiple sclerosis (MS) is the most common cause of optic neuritis in young adults (20-50 years), with a 50% risk of developing MS at 15 years after an episode.
  • Optic neuritis is the initial demyelinating event in ~20% of patients with MS.
  • The study ONTT (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial) demonstrated that MRI with demyelinating lesions is the main predictor of conversion to MS.
  • Trap: the presence of anti-MOG or anti-AQP4 antibodies suggests neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (NMOSD), not typical MS.

items.title: Uhthoff sign
items.contentHtml:
Uhthoff's sign

  • The Uhthoff sign consists of the transient worsening of visual acuity with increased body temperature (exercise, hot shower, fever).
  • Pathophysiology: heat exacerbates conduction block in demyelinated axons (en bloc conduction phenomenon dependent on temperature).
  • It is characteristic of demyelinating optic neuritis, but not pathognomonic; It can also be seen in other demyelinating neuropathies.
  • Trap: Uhthoff's sign does not indicate disease progression; It is reversible when cooled.

items.foco: Pupillary reflex: relative afferent pupillary defect
items.title: Relative afferent pupillary defect
items.contentHtml:
Pupillary reflex: relative afferent pupillary defect

  • The relative afferent pupillary defect (RPD) is evaluated with the swinging flashlight test.
  • In unilateral optic neuritis, the pupil of the affected eye paradoxically dilates when illuminated (consensual response preserved, afferent damaged).
  • The RPD is objective and highly sensitive to detect optic nerve damage, even if visual acuity is normal.
  • Trap: In bilateral symmetrical optic neuritis, the DPAR may be absent; Campimetry is required to confirm asymmetry.

items.title: eye fundus
items.contentHtml:
Fundum: normal or edematous papilla

  • In 65% of cases (retrobulbar optic neuritis), the fundus is normal because the inflammation is behind the lamina cribrosa.
  • In the remaining 35% (papillitis), papillary edema with blurred edges, venous engorgement and peripapillary hemorrhages are observed. occasional.
  • The presence of cotton-like exudates or optic atrophy (temporary pallor of the disc) suggests chronicity or alternative causes (AION, compressive).
  • Trap: a normal eye fundus does not rule out optic neuritis; The absence of edema does not imply less severity.

items.title: Campimetry
items.contentHtml:
Campimetry: central or centrocecal scotoma

  • The most typical campimetric defect in optic neuritis is the central scotoma or centrocecal (affects the fixation point and extends to the blind spot).
  • Altitudinal, arcuate or generalized defects can also be observed, but the central scotoma is the most characteristic of demyelination.
  • The Automated campimetry (Humphrey, Goldmann) is essential to document the defect and monitor recovery.
  • Trap: a pure altitudinal defect is more suggestive of arteritic or non-arteritic AION than of optic neuritis.

items.foco: MRI: enhancement of the optic nerve on T1 sequences with gadolinium
items.title: MRI with enhancement
items.contentHtml:
MRI: enhancement of the optic nerve on T1 sequences with gadolinium

  • The MRI of the orbits and brain with contrast shows enhancement of the optic nerve on T1 sequences with fat suppression after gadolinium, indicating active inflammation.
  • The enhancement can be segmental, longitudinal or nodular; the extent does not directly correlate with visual acuity.
  • Brain MRI evaluates periventricular, callosal, or juxtacortical demyelinating lesions to predict MS risk.
  • Trap: the absence of enhancement does not rule out optic neuritis if the MRI is performed >2 weeks after onset or if there is chronic atrophy.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis: arteritic and non-arteritic AION
items.title: Differential diagnosis with AION
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis: arteritic and non-arteritic AION

  • Arteritic AION (associated with giant cell arteritis): >50 years, temporal pain, mandibular claudication, elevated ESR/CRP, positive temporal artery biopsy.
  • Non-arteritic AION: lower altitudinal defect, small optic disc (crowded disc), no pain, factors vascular risk (HTN, DM).
  • In optic neuritis, pain with eye movements and dyschromatopsia are more prominent; the DPAR is similar.
  • Trap: arteritic AION requires urgent treatment with high-dose corticosteroids to prevent visual loss in the other eye; do not wait for MRI.

items.title: Acute treatment
items.contentHtml:
Acute treatment: intravenous methylprednisolone

  • First-line treatment is intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3-5 days, followed by oral prednisone in a descending regimen (according to ONTT).
  • It shortens the duration of pain and accelerates visual recovery, but does not improve the final visual result at 6 months compared to placebo.
  • The Oral prednisone alone in low doses (<60 mg/day) is contraindicated because it was associated with a higher recurrence rate in the ONTT.
  • Trap: in suspected NMOSD (anti-AQP4+), plasmapheresis is preferred if there is no response to corticosteroids.

items.foco: Prognosis: visual recovery in weeks, without treatment
items.title: Forecast
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis: visual recovery in weeks, without treatment

  • Spontaneous visual recovery begins within 2-3 weeks in most patients, even without treatment.
  • In the ONTT, 80% of patients recovered visual acuity ≥20/40 at 6 months; only 3% were left with vision <20/200.
  • dyschromatopsia and low-frequency contrast can persist as subtle sequelae.
  • Trap: the visual prognosis is worse in patients with anti-AQP4 (NMOSD) or anti-MOG antibodies, where recurrence and bilaterality are more frequent.

items.foco: McDonald criteria: useful for diagnosing MS
items.title: McDonald criteria
items.contentHtml:
McDonald criteria: useful for diagnosing MS

  • The McDonald criteria (2017) require demonstrating dissemination in space (≥2 lesions in ≥2 of 4 areas: periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) and in time (new T2/gadolinium+ lesion in follow-up, or simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions).
  • In unilateral optic neuritis, if there is ≥1 demyelinating lesion on MRI, dissemination in space is true; the presence of oligoclonal bands in CSF can replace the dissemination criterion in time.
  • Trap: McDonald's criteria do not apply to NMOSD; If suspected, request anti-AQP4 and anti-MOG antibodies.

items.foco: Devic syndrome: severe bilateral optic neuritis
items.title: Devic syndrome (NMOSD)
items.contentHtml:
Devic syndrome: severe bilateral optic neuritis

  • Devic syndrome or neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) is characterized by severe bilateral optic neuritis (acuity <20/200) and longitudinally extensive myelitis (≥3 vertebral segments).
  • Associated with anti-AQP4 antibodies. (aquaporin-4) in ~80% of cases; the rest may be anti-MOG+ or seronegative.
  • On MRI, optic neuritis is usually bilateral, with involvement of the chiasm and extensive enhancement; the cord shows hyperintense lesions on T2 that occupy >50% of the diameter.
  • Acute treatment: IV methylprednisolone; if unresponsive, plasmapheresis; prevention of recurrences with immunosuppressants (rituximab, mycophenolate).
